Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd set aside time during the week, maybe around 34 hours total, but I wasn't always consistent about when exactly I'd sit down. I preferred reading through materials and taking notes rather than watching videos endlessly. Some weeks I'd be more focused than others, but I generally just did what needed to be done without overthinking it.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was coming up. If there was a deadline approaching, I'd buckle down more, but otherwise I kind of spread things out. I didn't have a strict Monday-Wednesday-Friday schedule or anything like that. I just fit studying in around my other activities and commitments when I could.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I needed to check if something was due or to grab a reading. I wasn't someone who logged in every day just to browse around. It was more functional for me—get in, get what I need, get out. Sometimes the gamified tasks would prompt me to check in, which was kind of nice actually.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became something I did automatically every day. I'd remember to check it when I had to, but it wasn't like brushing my teeth or anything. The gamified elements made it slightly more interesting to engage with, but I wouldn't say it became a true habit for me.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful for me since that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs and go through them at my own pace, highlighting and taking notes. The discussion forums were okay too—I posted a few times and it helped to see how other people were thinking about the material.
Q6: I definitely didn't watch as many videos as I probably should have. They just take so long, and I felt like I could get the same information faster by reading. Some of the optional interactive modules I skipped too because they felt like busy work rather than actual learning. If something didn't seem directly relevant to assignments or exams, I probably ignored it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it clearly connected to what I'd be assessed on or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was confused about. The gamified tasks were actually decent because they gave me a sense of progress and small wins. But if something felt like it was just padding the course, I'd skip it without much guilt.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities definitely felt different. Required stuff I'd do even if I wasn't in the mood because I had to. Optional things I'd only do if I was feeling motivated or if they seemed genuinely interesting. With my motivation being what it is, optional usually meant I wouldn't get around to it unless there was some incentive.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the readings and worked through them on my laptop without being connected to the LMS. I also used my own notes extensively and sometimes looked things up on external websites to clarify concepts. A lot of my actual learning happened with materials spread out on my desk, not while logged into anything.
Q10: Most of my studying probably wouldn't show up in the LMS data. I'd download a PDF once and then read it multiple times over several days. I'd review my handwritten notes before exams. I'd think through problems while walking to class or lying in bed. None of that registers anywhere, but it's real studying.
Q11: Yeah, that was basically my standard approach. I'd grab the materials once, maybe skim through them online briefly, and then work with them offline for hours or even days. It was just more comfortable for me to study without being tethered to a website. I could focus better that way.
Q12: There were definitely times when I felt kind of down and it was harder to care about the course. I've been feeling a bit sad lately, and some weeks it was tough to muster the energy to engage. But I'm pretty resilient, so I'd usually bounce back and get through what needed to be done eventually.
Q13: Fatigue was probably the biggest factor for me. When I was tired from other activities or just feeling low, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS. I might log in once, grab what I needed, and then not come back for a while. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars also meant the LMS wasn't always my priority.
Q14: Oh definitely. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd technically be 'using' the LMS but not really absorbing anything. Those sessions felt kind of pointless in retrospect, but sometimes you just go through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, which is probably why my completion rate isn't great. Even when I knew something was important, if I wasn't feeling it, I'd push it off. It's not that I didn't care—I just struggled to find the motivation sometimes. I'd tell myself I'd do it later and then later wouldn't come.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main thing that got me to actually submit work. Without a deadline looming, I'd drift along doing minimal engagement. When something was due soon, I'd suddenly become much more focused and productive. It's not the healthiest pattern, but it's how I operated this semester.
Q17: Feedback did matter to me when I received it. If I got good feedback, it encouraged me to keep going with a similar approach. If the feedback pointed out issues, I'd try to adjust. But honestly, the feedback wasn't always timely enough to really change my behavior in the moment.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it didn't really push me to engage more than I naturally would. Having those gamified tasks was a nice touch—it gave me little goals to hit and made things slightly more interesting. But the overall structure was pretty standard, so I just did what I needed to get by.
Q19: The gamified elements made engagement a bit easier because there was something satisfying about completing them. What made it harder was when the course felt like a lot of disconnected pieces without clear direction. Also, my attendance being low meant I sometimes missed context that would have helped me engage better with online materials.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for things, trying to figure out what's important, or just clicking around without really knowing what to do. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. You can spend hours on an LMS being confused or distracted and have nothing to show for it at the end.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. I didn't log in much and my task completion wasn't great, but I still got a Merit because I studied effectively offline and did well on exams. The LMS activity doesn't capture reading downloaded materials or reviewing notes. Performance and online activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, which gives a rough picture of online behavior. But they miss everything that happens offline—the reading, the thinking, the note-taking. They also can't tell if someone was genuinely engaged or just going through the motions while distracted.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to check boxes. It's when you're curious about something and want to learn more, or when you're genuinely trying to improve. For me, engagement fluctuated—sometimes I was really into it, other times I was just surviving.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I did most of my meaningful studying offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. It wouldn't capture the times I discussed concepts with classmates in person or thought through problems on my own. It also wouldn't show that despite feeling sad sometimes, I pushed through and still managed to do reasonably well.
